---
name: define-terminology
description: 'Keep the words consistent across a document and a doc set. Pin down the terms a change introduces or renames, catch synonyms and drift where one thing is called several names, and seed a glossary. Use when drafting or reviewing docs, or when a change adds or changes user-facing names.'
---

# Define terminology

Inconsistent words make docs harder to trust and follow. The same thing called
three names, or one name used for three things, quietly confuses the reader. Pin
the terms down and keep them consistent across the document and the wider doc set.

## What to do

- **List the key terms** the change introduces, renames, or relies on — features,
  objects, actions, states, roles.
- **Choose one name per concept** and use it everywhere. Note names that were
  rejected so they don't creep back in.
- **Catch synonym drift** — the same concept referred to by different words across
  sections or documents.
- **Catch overloaded terms** — one word meaning different things in different
  places. Split or qualify them.
- **Match the reader's vocabulary** — prefer the term the audience already uses
  over an internal or code-level name (see write-for-the-reader).
- **Note terms that need confirmation** — where the right name is a decision only
  the human or an SME can make.

## Consistency across the set

- Check a new or changed term against how existing docs already use it. Don't
  introduce a second name for something already named.
- When a term is central or ambiguous, seed a short glossary entry so later docs
  can reuse it instead of reinventing the wording.
- When the doc set is large enough to drift, keep the agreed terms in one shared
  place (a glossary doc) so every later doc points at the same definitions.

Consistent terminology is a small discipline with an outsized effect on how
trustworthy and readable a doc set feels.
